name: Alocasia melo
aboutText: Alocasia melo is a spectacular dwarf jewel Alocasia endemic to the Sabah region of Borneo, where it grows specifically in ultramafic soils on rocky forest slopes. It is famous for its extremely thick, almost cardboard-like leaves with a deeply rugose, brain-like texture and a dark bluish-green to grey-green coloration. The leaves form a compact, low-growing rosette. Due to its specific native habitat, it requires excellent drainage and careful watering, but rewards growers with its unmatched sculptural beauty.

morphology.leafShape: Broadly ovate to peltate, deeply rugose
morphology.venation: Deeply sunken primary and secondary veins

fieldNotes.title: Borneo's Cardboard Curiosity
fieldNotes.author: Aroid Aaron
fieldNotes.content: Right then, kettle's on. A spot of tea always helps when contemplating Alocasia melo, the 'Rugosa Jewel.' This Borneo chap, formally described not long ago, hails from Sabah’s rocky, ultramafic slopes, making it a truly singular specimen. Its leaves are an utter marvel – so thick they feel like cardboard, deeply rugose, like a miniature brain sculpted from dark bluish-green leather. Absolutely no variegation, mind you, just that stunning texture. A proper dwarf rosette, it demands excellent drainage, a nod to its native habitat. Watering needs to be carefull, naturally. A bit fussy, perhaps, but the sculptural beauty it brings to the glasshouse makes the endeavour entirely worthwhile. One rather forgets the price paid for such a distinctive chunk of flora.
